1:
On Matthew 12:1 — the disciples plucking and eating the ears of corn on the sabbath — the witnesses read the opening of the great sabbath controversy.

The eastern and Latin fathers probe why Christ led them through the fields. Chrysostom answers that he “desired… to break the sabbath… but not absolutely” — “he broke it not without cause, but furnished a sufficient reason,” the disciples’ hunger, which “delivers them from all accusation”; he bids us “admire the disciples” who, “assailed by hunger… go not away from Christ.” Augustine notes the Jews charged “the breach of the sabbath rather than… theft,” for the Law allowed a hungry man to pluck (Deuteronomy 23:25); Hilary and Rabanus read the corn-field figuratively — the field “the world,” the hunger “their desire for the salvation of men.”

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators fix the occasion and the principle. Lightfoot and Barnes explain Luke’s “second-first sabbath” as the first after the second paschal day when the firstfruits sheaf was waved; “corn” means grain, not maize (Barnes, Robertson). Henry draws the doctrine that “works of necessity… are lawful on the sabbath day” — God “has many ways of bringing suitable provision to his people… while we are in the way of duty” — and that Christ would settle “a point… of use to his church in all ages.” The Reformed Calvin lays bare the Pharisees’ temper: it was “hypocrisy… that made them so exact in trifling matters, while they spared themselves in gross superstitions” (Matthew 23:23) — “malevolence and envy, still more than superstition.”

The witnesses read the scene as Christ’s deliberate correction of a corrupted sabbath. Henry notes the Jewish teachers had “interpreted it too strictly,” thinking “by their zeal in rituals… to atone for the looseness of their morals”; Christ would teach that “our Christian sabbath, though under the direction of the fourth commandment, is not under the injunctions of the Jewish elders.” Augustine adds the deeper figure — “no man passes into the body of Christ, until he has been stripped of his fleshly raiment” (Ephesians 4:22). The disciples’ rubbing the ears in their hands, Jerome observes, “is a proof of an austere life,” of men who “sought only simple food”; and Henry, that “while we are in the way of duty, Jehovah-jireh” — let God provide. The whole episode is set down to settle, by a case in fact, that “works of necessity are lawful on that day.”
2:
On Matthew 12:2 — “the Pharisees… said unto him, Behold, thy disciples do that which is not lawful to do upon the sabbath day” — the witnesses explain the precise charge.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground it in the rabbinic rules. Lightfoot notes “they do not contend about the thing itself, because it was lawful” (Deuteronomy 23:25), “but about the thing done on the sabbath”: to pluck the ears was “a kind of reaping,” a derivative of the thirty-nine forbidden works. Robertson agrees the disciples “plucking the heads of wheat… to the Pharisees was reaping and… rubbing them in their hands… was threshing.” Ellicott sets the scene with the watchers “come down from Jerusalem” (Luke 5:17), who now find the Teacher seeming to sanction “a distinct breach of the holiness of the day.”

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke marks the extreme of their superstition — the Jews in their wars “thought it a crime even to attempt to defend themselves on the Sabbath,” so that Pompey took Jerusalem — and draws the moral that “those who know not the spirit and design of the divine law are often superstitious to inhumanity, and indulgent to impiety.” “An intolerant and censorious spirit in religion,” he adds, “is one of the greatest curses.” The complaint, the witnesses agree, was brought not to learn but to accuse — the harmless act of hungry men “evil spoken of… by those who are zealous for their own inventions” (Henry).

The witnesses dwell on the spirit of the cavil. It was “but a dry breakfast,” Henry notes, “yet the Pharisees would not let them eat that in quietness”; they quarrelled not at the taking of another man’s corn — for “they were no great zealots for justice” — but at its being done on the sabbath. “It is no new thing,” he adds, “for the most harmless… actions of Christ’s disciples to be evil spoken of… especially by those who are zealous for their own inventions.” The detail Lightfoot supplies — that plucking ranked with reaping among the thirty-nine forbidden works, threshing with grinding — shows how a natural act of hunger was forced into the frame of a capital charge. Those, the witnesses conclude, “are no friends to Christ… who make that to be unlawful which God has not made to be so.”
3:
On Matthew 12:3 — “Have ye not read what David did, when he was an hungred?” — the witnesses read Christ’s first defence, drawn from a case the Pharisees themselves revered.

The grammatical-historical commentators recount the story. David, fleeing Saul, came to Ahimelech at Nob and, having no common bread, received the shewbread, “which by the law was appropriated to the priest” (Barnes; 1 Samuel 21:6; Leviticus 24:9). The act “had passed uncondemned,” proving “that in cases of necessity the laws did not bind a man.” Ellicott frames it as an argument a fortiori: would they “accuse David of sacrilege… in a case of urgent need”? If not, how condemn the disciples “for a far lighter transgression”? Robertson notes David did “what was not lawful” (ho ouk exon ēn), “precisely the charge made against the disciples.”

The Reformed Calvin lists this as the first of “five arguments” refuting the calumny. He takes for granted “that David was free from blame, because the Holy Spirit bestows commendation on the priest who allowed him to partake”; hence “the ceremonies of the Law are not violated where there is no infringement of godliness.” Jerome adds that “the preservation of man is a sacrifice acceptable to God,” Ahimelech “esteeming it a better action to deliver men from the danger of famine than to offer sacrifice.” Wesley sums the principle: “necessity was a sufficient plea for his transgressing the law in a higher instance.” The misattached Cyril fragment, on Christ as the true Vine buried in the garden, sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses note the force of the precedent in the Pharisees’ own esteem. David, says Chrysostom, was one “whose glory as a Prophet was great among the Jews,” so that the greater the man, “the greater is the defence of the disciples” — and yet even David’s companions, like the disciples, were no priests. Henry draws the rule: “that may be done in a case of necessity which may not be done at another time; there are laws which necessity has not,” for “hunger… will break through stone walls.” What bore David out, he adds, “was not his dignity… but his hunger” — for Uzziah, invading the priest’s office in pride, was struck with leprosy (2 Chronicles 26:16). If the very letter of a ritual institution yielded to David’s want, “much more might the tradition of the elders be dispensed with.”
4:
On Matthew 12:4 — “how he entered into the house of God, and did eat the shewbread, which was not lawful for him to eat… but only for the priests” — the witnesses fill out the detail of the precedent.

The grammatical-historical commentators locate and explain the act. “The house of God” was not the temple, “the temple not being then built,” but the tabernacle at Nob, where Ahimelech ministered (Gill, Ellicott; 1 Samuel 21:6). The shewbread, Ellicott and Wesley note, was “twelve loaves, in two rows of six,” offered “every sabbath day” (Leviticus 24:5–9) and reserved, when removed, “for the exclusive use of the priests.” The lesson Ellicott draws is the “general principle that when positive commands and necessities involving the good of man come into collision, the latter… must prevail.”

The witnesses note both the force and the type of the example. Chrysostom marks that “the greater was he who did this, the greater is the defence of the disciples,” for David, “though a Prophet,” was not a priest, and “they that were with him” still less. Yet the bread itself, Wesley observes, “represented the twelve tribes of Israel”; and Clarke (with Parkhurst) sees in it a type of Christ, “first presented as a sacrifice… and then becoming spiritual food” to those who are “spiritual priests to God” (1 Peter 2:5). The precedent stands, on every reading, as proof that the letter of a ceremonial law yields to a genuine necessity.

The witnesses press the lesson that necessity is a law to itself. Christ takes for granted, Calvin notes, “that David was free from blame, because the Holy Spirit bestows commendation on the priest who allowed him” the holy bread — whence “the ceremonies of the Law are not violated where there is no infringement of godliness.” The shewbread was “most holy to Aaron and his sons” (Exodus 29:33), yet “the exception of a case of necessity… was implied in that and all other ritual institutions” (Henry). The argument disarms the Pharisees on their own ground: they honoured David and blamed not Ahimelech, allowing “hunger” as a valid plea — and so, Jerome urges, must “approve in the Apostles the same plea which you approve in others.” The disciples’ lighter act, rubbing ears of corn, is thus the more easily justified.
5:
On Matthew 12:5 — “have ye not read in the law, how that on the sabbath days the priests in the temple profane the sabbath, and are blameless?” — the witnesses read Christ’s second, stronger defence.

The grammatical-historical commentators detail the priestly labour. On the sabbath the priests slew the two additional lambs (Numbers 28:9), kindled fire, and burned the sacrifices — work which “for other persons… would have been profaning the sabbath” (Barnes), the very kindling of fire being forbidden (Exodus 35:3) — “yet they were blameless.” Lightfoot cites the rabbinic maxim, “there is no sabbatism at all in the Temple.” Chrysostom marks how much Christ here gathers — “the place, in the Temple… the time, on the sabbath… the setting aside the Law, in the word profane” — and that this case, unlike David’s, “is done every sabbath, and by the Priests, and according to the Law,” so that “by the strict law the disciples are to be held blameless.”

The Reformed Calvin draws the principle of harmony: “the duties of piety are in no degree inconsistent with each other,” and if the temple-service “sanctifies manual operations,” much more does “the holiness of the true and spiritual” service exempt its worshippers from blame. He grants the word “profane” is “accommodated to his hearers,” admitting as true “what might appear to be so” to the ignorant, and resting content with proving “that the labors performed in the temple are not offensive to God.” Wesley draws the bridge to the next verse: “if the Sabbath must give way to the temple, much more must it give way to me.” The misattached Origen fragment, on the divorced wife and the returning husband, sits here as a stray.

The witnesses mark how this second case strengthens the first. David’s was a single deed, “done but once… and a case of necessity” (Chrysostom); but the priests profane the sabbath “every sabbath, and… according to the Law” — so that the disciples are blameless “not only by indulgence… but by the strict law.” The point, Calvin presses, is that “the exercises of godliness are not opposed to each other, but agree well together”: the rest of the sabbath was never meant to forbid the very service of God for which it was kept. Wesley draws the bridge plainly — “if therefore the Sabbath must give way to the temple, much more must it give way to me.” The labour of slaying, flaying, and burning the sacrifices, criminal in another, was in the priests an act of worship; and so the disciples’ service of the Lord of the temple cannot be a breach of the day.
6:
On Matthew 12:6 — “but I say unto you, that in this place is one greater than the temple” — the witnesses hear a claim to a dignity above the holiest place.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the reading and its force. The better text is neuter — “something greater than the temple” (Ellicott, Robertson) — which Ellicott takes of “the body of the Son of Man” as “the truest, highest temple of God,” with the wider truth that “man, as such… is greater than any material temple” (1 Corinthians 6:19). Barnes refers it directly to Christ: “I have power over the laws… none but God can authorize men to do a thing contrary to the Divine laws” — and joins Mark’s word, “the sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath” (Mark 2:27). Clarke hears an allusion to Malachi 3:1: by claiming to be greater than the temple, Christ “asserts that he was God.”

The Puritan Henry presses the comparison from the priests: “if the temple-service would justify what the priests did… the service of Christ would much more justify the disciples in… their attendance upon him.” The Jews held the temple in such veneration that “it sanctified the gold” (Matthew 23:17); but Christ, “in a corn-field, was greater than the temple,” for in him dwelt “all the fulness of the Godhead bodily.” The lesson he draws is that whatever is done “in the name of Christ, and as unto him… shall be graciously accepted of God, however it may be censured… by men.”

The witnesses gather the verse as the hinge of the whole defence. The temple, Henry notes, the Jews held in such veneration that “it sanctified the gold” (Matthew 23:17) and “Stephen was accused for blaspheming that holy place” (Acts 6:13); yet here in a corn-field stood one greater than it. Whether read of Christ’s person, of his body as the true temple, or of the worth of man (Ellicott), the conclusion is the same: if the temple-service justified the priests’ labour, “the service of Christ would much more justify the disciples.” Barnes draws the proof of deity — “none but God can authorize men to do a thing contrary to the Divine laws” — and joins Mark’s great word that “the sabbath was made for man, and not man for the sabbath” (Mark 2:27), so that “his necessities… are not to bend to that which was made for him.”
7:
On Matthew 12:7 — “but if ye had known what this meaneth, I will have mercy, and not sacrifice, ye would not have condemned the guiltless” — the witnesses read the third argument, drawn from Hosea.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the rebuke. The teachers of the Law “had failed to catch the meaning of the simplest utterances of the prophets” (Ellicott; Hosea 6:6): “moral and not positive duties… made up the true life of religion,” and because they had “inverted the right relation of the two” they condemned the guiltless. Robertson notes it is “a pertinent prophecy that had escaped the notice of the sticklers for ceremonial literalness,” quoted as in Matthew 9:13. Henry draws the lessons: that “ignorance is the cause of our rash… censures,” and that “it is not enough… to know the scriptures, but we must labour to know the meaning of them” — ignorance “especially shameful in those who take upon them to teach others.”

The Reformed Calvin meets the question why God, who enjoined sacrifice, declares himself indifferent to it. “External rites are of no value in themselves, and are demanded by God in so far only as they are directed to their proper object”; by comparison with “deeds of kindness” they are “inferior… in actual value.” The hypocrites “imitate piety by outward signs, and yet pervert it” by “confining their laborious efforts to the carnal worship alone.” Clarke gathers from the whole passage “four ways in which positive laws may cease to oblige” — by necessity, by a superior law, by mercy, and by “the dispensation… of the Lawgiver.” The misattached JFB note on persecution sits here as a stray.

The witnesses make the verse a rebuke of misplaced zeal. The Pharisees, exact in ceremony, had never learned “what this meaneth” — that God “will have mercy, and not sacrifice” (Hosea 6:6) — and so “condemned the guiltless.” Henry draws three lessons: “ignorance is the cause of our rash and uncharitable censures”; “it is not enough… to know the scriptures, but we must labour to know the meaning of them”; and such ignorance is “especially shameful in those who take upon them to teach others.” The rest of the sabbath, he adds, “was ordained for man’s good… in favour of the body” (Deuteronomy 5:14), and “no law must be construed so as to contradict its own end.” Had they known mercy, they “would have been sorry that” the disciples “were forced to do this to satisfy their hunger.” Ceremony, the witnesses agree, must yield to the moral and the merciful.
8:
On Matthew 12:8 — “for the Son of man is Lord even of the sabbath day” — the witnesses read the crowning argument and its claim of authority.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress both the title and the power. “The Son of man,” Gill insists, is not “any man” — “no mere man is lord of any law” — but Christ, who “as he was the institutor of the sabbath… could dispense with it.” Ellicott notes the title’s significance: what Christ claims he claims “as the representative of humanity, acting… in its name,” even while it is “a special and absolute prerogative.” Robertson observes that this claim “to be master of the Sabbath… angered them extremely,” involving “the claim of Messiahship.” Clarke finds the proof of his Godhead, and in the change to “the Lord’s day” (Revelation 1:10) a sign that “Christ is… the truth and completion of it.”

The Reformed Calvin distinguishes this from the “greater than the temple” of verse 6: there Christ pleaded his personal holiness, but here “he declares that he has received authority to exempt his followers from the necessity of observing the Sabbath,” relaxing it “as other legal ceremonies.” “Out of Christ the bondage of the Law is wretched, from which he alone delivers those on whom he bestows… the Spirit of adoption” (Romans 8:15). Henry adds that the Lord of the sabbath “was authorized to make… that day… the Lord’s day,” and that, this being so, “the day and all the work of it should be dedicated to him.” The misattached Cyril fragment on the willing Passion sits here as a stray.

The witnesses crown the argument with the claim of lordship. The same law as all the rest, Henry notes, “is put into the hand of Christ, to be altered, enforced, or dispensed with”: it was “by the Son” that God “instituted the sabbath in innocency” and “gave the ten commandments,” and as “Lord of the sabbath” he “was authorized to make… the Lord’s day.” Lightfoot recalls that before the Sanhedrin (John 5) Christ pleaded the same dominion, “invested… with the same authority… in respect of the New Testament, as the Father” in the Old. The works of necessity Christ here authorizes, all agree, are “really such, and not a pretended… necessity”; and the very fact that he expounds rather than abolishes the commandment “plainly shows that it was to be perpetual” — the exception confirming the rule.
9:
On Matthew 12:9 — “and when he was departed thence, he went into their synagogue” — the witnesses pass to the second sabbath controversy, the withered hand.

The Latin and eastern fathers set the scene. Jerome notes the Pharisees, foiled in accusing the disciples, “seek to bring false accusation against Himself”; he reports the Nazarene Gospel’s detail that the man was a bricklayer who pleaded, “I was a builder… restore me… that I may not disgracefully beg my bread.” Augustine carefully harmonizes the Evangelists — the cure was on “another sabbath” (Luke), and the Pharisees first asked, “Is it lawful to heal?” before Christ, “understanding their thoughts,” set the man “in the midst” and questioned them. Chrysostom marks “the tender bowels of the Lord” in setting the man forth, “that by the sight He might subdue them.” Hilary and Rabanus read the withered hand allegorically — “the human race in its barrenness of good works,” healed by “the stretching out of the innocent hand on the Cross.”

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators draw the spiritual and practical sense. Henry notes the cure “is recorded for the sake of the time when it was wrought, on the sabbath” — “the work of mercy… done by himself” matching “the work of necessity… done by the disciples.” The Gloss draws the sharp contrast: the Pharisees “profane the sabbath by works of covetousness who were charging Him with profaning it by works of charity,” for it is “only evil deeds from which we ought to rest” (Leviticus 23:3). And the withered hand, all agree, is an emblem: “by nature our hands are withered… it is Christ only, by the power of his grace, that cures us.”

The witnesses read the move into the synagogue as Christ pressing on with his work. Henry warns that “we must not, for the sake of private feuds… draw back from public worship”: though the Pharisees had cavilled, “yet he went into their synagogue,” lest Satan “by sowing discord… prevail to drive” any “from the communion of the faithful.” The withered hand is read by the fathers as a standing emblem: Hilary, that “the work of human salvation is done by the Apostles” whose hand was made “able to the ministry”; Rabanus, that it figures “the human race in its barrenness of good works… healed by the stretching out of the innocent hand on the Cross.” And the Gloss draws the sharp rule for the day: “in the everlasting rest, we shall rest only from evil, and not from good” (Leviticus 23:3).
10:
On Matthew 12:10 — “and, behold, there was a man which had his hand withered. And they asked him… Is it lawful to heal on the sabbath days? that they might accuse him” — the witnesses note the affliction and the snare.

The grammatical-historical commentators describe both. The hand — Luke’s “right hand” (Gill) — was “dried up,” the man, by the Nazarene tradition, one who “got his bread by his hand labour,” so “the case was the more affecting.” The question, Lightfoot insists, was “not so much the words of inquirers, as deniers,” for the rabbinic rules forbade nearly all sabbath remedies — one might swallow oil for a sore throat but not gargle it. Ellicott marks the two facts implied: that they “expected our Lord to heal,” his sympathy passing “into act,” and that they had resolved, if he did, to “make it the ground of a definite accusation” — for the casuistry allowed healing only “in cases of life and death,” and the “withered hand, a permanent infirmity,” did not qualify.

The Reformed Calvin exposes the absurdity of the question. They ought to have considered “whether it was a work of God, or of man, to restore a withered hand by a mere touch”; when God appointed the sabbath “he did not… impose upon himself any restraint from performing operations” on it. “It was excessive folly… to call this in question, and thus to prescribe rules for God himself, and to restrain the freedom of his operations.” The malice that watched for an accusation, all the witnesses agree, betrayed itself: they cared more “to hurt the fame of Christ, than to see this person made whole” (Chrysostom).

The witnesses dwell on the malice that watched for an accusation. Henry notes “they observed Christ began to take notice of” the man, “and knew it was usual for him to be found of those that sought him not, and therefore… they anticipated his goodness” with their question — a snare set “in the way of doing good.” The dilemma was contrived: if Christ said it lawful to heal, they would charge him with breaking the fourth commandment; if not, with the “partiality” of having just justified the disciples. Yet, Henry urges, “it is past dispute, that it is lawful for prophets to heal, for him to heal who discovered a divine power… in all he did” — “did ever any ask, whether it is lawful for God to heal?” Their question, the witnesses agree, sprang not from a desire to learn but “that they might accuse him” (Chrysostom).
11:
On Matthew 12:11 — “What man shall there be among you, that shall have one sheep, and if it fall into a pit on the sabbath day, will he not lay hold on it, and lift it out?” — the witnesses read Christ’s appeal to their own practice.

The grammatical-historical commentators ground it in the rabbinic canons. Lightfoot and Clarke cite the rule, “we must take a tender care of the goods of an Israelite”: if a beast fell into a pit on the sabbath, the owner might bring “clothes and litter,” or lift it out, “rather than lose a sheep” (Henry). Clarke draws the sharp moral: “self-interest is a very decisive casuist, and removes abundance of scruples in a moment” — the man would do for his property on the sabbath what he forbade Christ to do for a man. Henry notes Christ “says it for our sakes” (1 Corinthians 9:10), and that the sheep is “preferred far before” nothing, but man far before the sheep.

The Reformed Calvin presses that to withhold help is itself sin. “He who takes away the life of a man is held to be a criminal; and there is little difference between manslaughter and the conduct of him who does not concern himself about relieving a person in distress.” So Christ “indirectly charges them with endeavoring, under the pretense of a holy act, to compel him” to sin — “for sin is committed… not only by him who does anything contrary to the Law, but also by him who neglects his duty.” The argument, drawn from their own admitted custom, leaves them “completely refuted… that nothing could be more unreasonable than to pronounce a man, who imitated God, to be a transgressor of the Sabbath.”

The witnesses note how Christ turns their own practice against them. The argument, all agree, is drawn from a thing they would unhesitatingly do: a man with one sheep, “the loss of which would not be very great,” would yet “lift it out” of a pit on the sabbath, “rather than lose a sheep” (Henry). Their own rabbinic canons (Lightfoot) provided for it. Clarke draws the searching moral that “self-interest is a very decisive casuist,” quick to find exceptions for a beast while denying mercy to a man. Calvin presses the deeper point that mere inaction can be sin: “he who takes away the life of a man is held to be a criminal; and there is little difference between manslaughter and the conduct of him who… does not… relieve… a person in distress.” So the Pharisees, under “the pretense of a holy act,” would in effect compel Christ to the sin of neglect.
12:
On Matthew 12:12 — “How much then is a man better than a sheep? Wherefore it is lawful to do well on the sabbath days” — the witnesses draw out the conclusion of the argument.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the worth of man. Barnes: “if you would show an act of kindness to a brute beast… how much more important… to one made in the image of God… for whom the Saviour came to die.” Robertson presses the question to its root — “by how much does a human being differ from a sheep? That is the question which Christian civilization has not even yet adequately answered” — “the poor pettifogging Pharisees are left in the pit.” Henry observes that “man, in respect of his being… is a great deal better… than the best of the brute creatures,” capable “of knowing, loving, and glorifying God,” rebuking those “more solicitous for… their horses and dogs than of God’s poor.”

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke names the form of the argument — “argumentum ad hominem… taken on their own ground, and confuted on their own maxims and conduct” — and turns it on the professing Christian who will “do more for a beast of burden or pleasure than… for a man for whom Christ died.” The conclusion, all agree, recasts their narrow question: they had asked “is it lawful to heal?” and Christ proves “it is lawful to do well” (Henry) — works of mercy, done “from a principle of love and charity, with humility and self-denial,” being among the proper duties of the day.

The witnesses make the verse a charter of mercy for the day. Christ proves, says Henry, that “it is lawful to do well on the sabbath days” — recasting their narrow “is it lawful to heal?” into the broad principle that works of love are proper to it. There are, he adds, “more ways of doing well upon sabbath days, than by the duties of God’s immediate worship”: “attending the sick, relieving the poor, helping those who are fallen into sudden distress” — done “from a principle of love and charity, with humility and self-denial.” The worth of man is the ground: he is “a reasonable creature, capable of knowing, loving, and glorifying God,” so that “the sacrifice of a sheep could… not atone for the sin of a soul.” The Pharisees, the witnesses note, were “more solicitous… for their horses and dogs than of God’s poor.”
13:
On Matthew 12:13 — “Then saith he to the man, Stretch forth thine hand. And he stretched it forth; and it was restored whole, like as the other” — the witnesses dwell on the command, the obedience, and the proof of deity.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the “remarkable commandment.” Barnes: “the man might have said that he had no strength… yet, being commanded, it was his duty to obey” — so the sinner is to obey God’s commands, for “God will give us strength, if there is a disposition to obey.” Clarke presses that “faith disregards apparent impossibilities, where there is a command and promise of God”: “the effort to believe is, often, that faith by which the soul is healed.” The cure, wrought “with a word, without even a touch,” left “the Sabbath unbroken, even according to their most rigid interpretation” — and Clarke adds, “what but the omnipotence of the living God could have, in a moment, restored this withered hand?” Robertson hopes the man “stretched it forth, straight… towards the Pharisees who were watching.”

The Puritan Henry reads the spiritual significancy throughout. “By nature our hands are withered… utterly unable… to do any thing that is good”; Christ “cures us… by putting life into the dead soul,” working “in us both to will and to do.” The command to stretch forth the hand — which the man could no more do of himself than Lazarus could rise — carries “a promise of grace… given by the word” (Proverbs 1:23), so that those who perish are “inexcusable,” and those who are saved owe all “to the power and grace of Christ.” The two cases together — necessity and mercy — thus settle, by Christ’s own example, “what may be done on the Sabbath.”

The witnesses make the cure a parable of grace. Christ bids the man “stretch forth” the very hand he could not move — a command no more absurd, Henry notes, than bidding Lazarus come forth — because “with the command, there is a promise of grace which is given by the word” (Proverbs 1:23). So “God’s commands to us to do the duty which of ourselves we are not able to do” are just, the man’s obedience being his only contribution, yet all the cure being Christ’s. Clarke marks that the healing “with a word… without even a touch” left the sabbath unbroken “even according to their most rigid interpretation,” and proved his Godhead, for “what but the omnipotence of the living God could have, in a moment, restored” it. The two cases together — the corn-field and the synagogue, necessity and mercy — thus determine, by Christ’s own example, “what may be done on the Sabbath.”
14:
On Matthew 12:14 — “then the Pharisees went out, and held a council against him, how they might destroy him” — the witnesses note the murderous turn of the conflict.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the crisis. Barnes and Robertson note that the Pharisees, foiled in argument and stung by envy, “bolted out of the synagogue and actually conspired with their hated rivals, the Herodians” (Mark 3:6) how to “put Jesus to death.” Ellicott observes that if these were the Pharisees from Jerusalem, the council bore on “our Lord’s future work” the more weightily. Henry calls it “the cursed malice of the Pharisees”: their real grievance was that his doctrine was “opposite to their pride… and worldly interest,” though they “pretended… displeasure at his breaking the sabbath” — “it is no new thing to see the vilest practices cloaked with the most specious pretences.”

The Reformed Calvin marvels at “the obstinate… rage which drives the wicked to oppose God”: “the most distinguished teachers of the Law… are engaged, like robbers, in contriving murder.” The Latin and eastern fathers, whose notes here look forward to the Isaiah prophecy soon to be quoted, mark the meekness of Christ’s response. Hilary: he “withdrew, that He might be far from the counsels of the evil-hearted”; Jerome: “that He might remove from the Pharisees all opportunity of sin.” Chrysostom reads the “bruised reed he shall not break” of Christ’s power and mercy — it was “as easy for Him to break them all, as to break a reed,” yet he spared them.

The witnesses measure the depth of the Pharisees’ turn to murder. Henry marks “their policy” in taking counsel together “in a close cabal,” and “their cruelty” in taking counsel “not to imprison or banish him, but to destroy him” — “to be the death of him who came that we might have life.” What an indignity, he adds, to “run him down as an outlaw… and the plague of his country, who was… the Glory of his people Israel.” The Catena fathers, whose notes here pass into the Isaiah prophecy that follows, mark his answering meekness: Chrysostom, that he could have broken the Pharisees “as a bruised reed” yet spared them, his power restrained by mercy until “judgment” should be sent “to victory.” The plot of the rulers, the witnesses agree, only drew out the gentleness foretold of the Servant of the Lord.
15:
On Matthew 12:15 — “but when Jesus knew it, he withdrew himself from thence: and great multitudes followed him; and he healed them all” — the witnesses read both his prudence and his unwearied mercy.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress the wisdom of the withdrawal. Barnes notes “he knew that his hour was not yet come,” and so “acted the part of Christian prudence,” retiring “to the sea” (Mark 3:7) and entering a boat to address the crowds — “no scene could be more sublime.” Clarke draws the maxim: “yield to the stream when you cannot stem it,” a man of God departing “when he finds the obstacles… invincible.” Robertson notes Jesus “read their very thoughts… plain to any one who saw their angry countenances.” “He healed them all” means, all agree, “all that had need of healing” and sought it.

The Puritan Henry draws the example. Christ withdrew “to decline, not his work, but his danger”; he “could have secured himself by miracle, but chose… the ordinary way of flight,” submitting “to the sinless infirmities of our nature,” and giving “an example to his own rule” (Matthew 10:23). His retirements were “filled up with business”: when the Pharisees drove him out, “the common people crowded after him,” and he was their “Physician-general… with healing under his wings.” Though the rulers persecuted him, “he did not let the people fare the worse” — teaching that “though some are unkind to us, we must not on that account be unkind to others.”

The witnesses commend Christ’s union of prudence with unwearied good. He withdrew, Henry notes, “not for his own ease, nor… to leave off his work”; his “retirements were filled up with business,” and he was “even then doing good, when he was forced to flee.” Thus he gave his ministers an example, “to do what they can, when they cannot do what they would, and to continue teaching, even when they are removed into corners.” The crowds that followed him out, “unbiased… by the pomp of the world,” were so zealous they would follow “whithersoever he went”; and he “received them and healed them all” — “a Physician-general… with healing under his wings.” The persecution of the rulers, the witnesses observe, did not make the people “fare the worse”: “though some are unkind to us, we must not on that account be unkind to others.”
16:
On Matthew 12:16 — “and charged them that they should not make him known” — the witnesses weigh the reasons for the imposed silence.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it as prudence in a time of danger. Lightfoot notes it was “not that he refused to heal… nor only to shun popular applause; but because he would keep himself hid from those who would not acknowledge him” — the same intent as his teaching by parables (Matthew 13:13). Barnes: “he wished to avoid their plots, and to save his life.” Ellicott, taking the generalised command, looks for a wider reason: “the hour of final conflict… had not yet come, and Jesus would not hasten it,” seeking “to work while it was yet day” and to avoid “premature occasions of offence” — again an example of being “wise as serpents” (Matthew 10:16).

The Reformed Calvin notes the charge did not aim to suppress his fame wholly but “to allow it to strike root, that it might bring forth abundant fruit at the proper season”; Christ “was manifested gradually… not revealed in his true character until the time appointed” (Galatians 4:2), and even “in this concealment his glory continues to shine.” The Puritan Henry adds the lessons of prudence, righteous judgment, and humility: “wise and good men… covet to do good, yet are far from coveting to have it talked of… because it is God’s acceptance, not men’s applause, that they aim at” — the reverse of those “who did all their works to be seen of men.”

The witnesses gather the threefold reading of the imposed silence. Henry weighs it as prudence — doing good “with as little noise as possible, to avoid offence… and peril” (Matthew 10:16) — as righteous judgment on Pharisees “unworthy to hear of any more of his miracles,” and as humility, that we “not… proclaim our own… usefulness.” Calvin adds the purpose of letting his fame “strike root” for fruit “at the proper season,” since Christ “was manifested gradually… not revealed in his true character until the time appointed” (Galatians 4:2). The deepest note Calvin sounds is that even where wicked men “do their utmost to extinguish the glory of God,” God turns it the other way, so that “in this very concealment his glory continues to shine, and even bursts forth… into its full splendor.”
17:
On Matthew 12:17 — “that it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet” — the witnesses note that Christ’s very reticence fulfilled prophecy.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify the text and its point. The quotation is of Isaiah 42:1–4, “a very free reproduction of the Hebrew” (Robertson). Barnes draws out the corrective: “the Jews, and the disciples also, at first, expected that the Messiah would be a conqueror”; when they saw him “retiring before” his enemies, “it was contrary to all their previous notions,” and Matthew shows by this quotation that “their conceptions of him had been wrong” — he was predicted “peaceful, retiring, and strengthening the feeble.” Gill notes the prophecy belongs to the Messiah, “as the Jews themselves” own.

The Reformed Calvin presses the deeper design: Matthew intends “to show, that the glory of Christ’s divinity ought not to be the less admired, because it appeared under a vail of infirmity.” The flesh “is constantly longing for outward display,” and so the Spirit declared the Messiah would be “totally different from earthly kings,” who “produce great noises… and fill cities… with commotion.” God “appointed for his Son a low and mean appearance,” not by accident “but in consequence of a decree of Heaven” — whence “he who is offended by the low condition of Christ… is unworthy of salvation.” We are “not at liberty to imagine to ourselves a Christ that corresponds to our fancy,” but must embrace him “as he is offered by the Father.”

The witnesses dwell on Matthew’s purpose in citing Isaiah. The retirement that might have looked like defeat was, Henry notes, the fulfilling of the word, “that though he was eclipsed, the word of God might be… glorified.” The prophecy’s scope is “to show how mild and quiet, and yet how successful,” the Servant should be — correcting the dream of “a pompous Saviour.” Calvin presses that Matthew would have us not despise “the glory of Christ’s divinity… because it appeared under a vail of infirmity”; the flesh “is constantly longing for outward display,” but God appointed his Son “a low and mean appearance… in consequence of a decree of Heaven.” We are “not at liberty to imagine to ourselves a Christ that corresponds to our fancy,” the witnesses conclude, “but ought simply to embrace him as he is offered by the Father” — for “he who is offended by the low condition of Christ… is unworthy of salvation.”
18:
On Matthew 12:18 — “Behold my servant, whom I have chosen; my beloved, in whom my soul is well pleased… he shall shew judgment to the Gentiles” — the witnesses unfold the Father’s testimony to the Messiah.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators expound the titles. Christ is God’s “servant” in the work of redemption, taking “the form of a servant” (Philippians 2:7); his “chosen,” for “none but Christ… able to do the Redeemer’s work” (Henry; Ephesians 1:4); his “beloved,” declared so “by a voice from heaven” (Matthew 3:17); and one in whom God is “well pleased,” so that “all the interest which fallen man has… in God is grounded upon… God’s well-pleasedness in” him (John 14:6). Ellicott notes the mysterious “servant of the Lord” of Isaiah found “its highest fulfilment in the Son of Man,” and that “judgment” includes “the work of a king, as teaching… righteousness” to the Gentiles — St. Matthew’s record, though for Jews, being “emphatically a Gospel for the Gentiles.”

The Reformed Calvin presses that God “points out by the finger” the person he sends, that we not “measure Christ by our own sense.” The delight of the Father’s soul dwells in Christ, and “in his person God the Father embraces in his love the whole Church”; the promised “judgment” is the kingdom of God spread “throughout the whole world,” Christ both prescribing what is right and inwardly forming “the hearts of men, by the grace of his Spirit.” The misattached Cyril fragment, on the one Spirit whose “operations” are various, sits beside the verse as a stray on “I will put my Spirit upon him.”

The witnesses unfold the Father’s fourfold testimony. Christ is the chosen Servant — “equal to the undertaking,” for “none but Christ… able to do the Redeemer’s work, or fit to wear the Redeemer’s crown” (Henry); the Beloved, between whom and the Father there was “before all time an eternal… interchanging of love” (Proverbs 8:30); the one in whom God is “well pleased,” so that “all the interest… fallen man… can have in God” is grounded on it (Ephesians 1:6). To him the Father promised the Spirit, “not by measure” (John 3:34) — “wherever he confers his love, he confers somewhat of his likeness” — and success, that “he should show judgment to the Gentiles.” Calvin: “in his person God the Father embraces in his love the whole Church,” for “his love will never come to us till it first begin with the Head.”
19:
On Matthew 12:19 — “he shall not strive, nor cry; neither shall any man hear his voice in the streets” — the witnesses dwell on the meekness of the Messiah’s working.

The grammatical-historical commentators read it of his calm, unostentatious manner. Gill notes “he shall not strive” in “a wrangling way… for the sake of victory,” nor “cry” with “reviling and opprobrious language”; Barnes: “he shall be meek, and retiring… not seek publicity and popularity.” Ellicott marks the “pervading calmness” that “stood out in marked contrast to the wrangling of Jewish scribes, the violence of Roman officers,” and of “false prophets and leaders of revolt, such as Judas of Galilee.” Henry: “his kingdom was spiritual, and therefore not to be advanced by force… the kingdom of God is not in word, but in power.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out why this mildness is gracious. Christ “mildly and voluntarily accommodates himself to” our capacity; “were Christ to appear in his glory… it would altogether swallow us up.” That “the gentleness of Christ may awaken reverence,” we must consider “how necessary that gentleness must be” to creatures conscious of their weakness. Wesley notes the clauses “rise above each other, expressing a still higher degree of humility and gentleness.” Yet Calvin adds the caution, anticipating the next verse, that mildness is not to be shown “indiscriminately towards all”: the stubborn “need to have their hardness beaten violently with a hammer.” The misattached JFB note (on Luke 21:18) sits here as a stray.

The witnesses contrast the Servant’s stillness with the world’s clamour. “Christ and his kingdom come not with observation” (Henry; Luke 17:20): “he made no public entry, had no harbingers to proclaim him King,” but “spake in a still small voice, which was alluring to all, but terrifying to none” — coming down “silently like the dew.” His kingdom being “spiritual… not to be advanced by force… or by high pretensions,” Calvin draws the gracious lesson: this very gentleness, by which Christ “accommodates himself to our capacity,” is what “the world everywhere despises,” though “were Christ to appear in his glory… it would altogether swallow us up.” Yet he warns, with the prophet’s own distinction, that mildness is not for all alike: the stubborn, who “spread darkness… must have their smoke dispelled and their flame extinguished.”
20:
On Matthew 12:20 — “a bruised reed shall he not break, and smoking flax shall he not quench, till he send forth judgment unto victory” — the witnesses dwell on the tenderness of Christ and divide over whom the figures denote.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the bruised reed and smoking flax as the contrite and the weak. Barnes: emblems “of the soul, broken and contrite… weeping… for transgression,” and of “the languishing graces of the people of God” — Christ will “cherish the feeble flame, minister the oil of grace, and kindle it into a blaze.” Clarke notes Christ “quenches not faint desires after salvation, even in the worst… of men.” Ellicott feels the publican-apostle “had found their fulfilment in his own… experience of the profound tenderness of his Master.” Henry offers both readings — of Christ’s “patience in bearing with the wicked” (he could break the Pharisees “as a bruised reed”) and, rather, of his “power and grace in bearing up the weak”: “the day of small things is the day of precious things” (Zechariah 4:10).

The Latin fathers read the figures of Jew and Gentile. Hilary takes the bruised reed of the Gentiles “not to be broken, but… reserved for salvation,” the smoking flax of the spark not quite quenched in Israel; Jerome offers the reverse — the bruised reed the patiently-endured Jews, the smoking flax the Gentiles raised “from a small spark… into a mighty flame.” The Reformed Calvin cherishes the weak but presses the distinction: “judgment” reaches “victory” only through struggle, for “victory is not obtained in any other way than by fighting,” the obstinate having their smoke dispelled and flame extinguished.

The witnesses cherish the tenderness of this verse above all. “The day of small things is the day of precious things” (Henry; Zechariah 4:10): the reed bruised shall be “supported, and made… strong as a cedar,” the candle newly lighted, “though it only smokes… shall not be blown out, but blown up.” Young beginners are “weak as a bruised reed,” their corruptions “as smoking flax”; yet Christ “deals very tenderly with those who have true grace, though they be weak in it” (Isaiah 40:11; Hebrews 5:2) — remembering “not only that we are dust, but that we are flesh.” And the issue is sure: the “judgment” he shows shall be “brought forth… to victory,” for “grace shall get the upper hand of corruption, and shall at length be perfected in glory” — “great is the truth, and will prevail.”
21:
On Matthew 12:21 — “and in his name shall the Gentiles trust” — the witnesses read the great end of the Messiah’s mild and victorious working.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the textual form and its sense. Where the Hebrew has “the isles shall wait for his law” (Isaiah 42:4), Matthew follows the Septuagint; but “the difference… is not so great,” Gill notes, since “the isles” denote “the Gentiles, or… nations,” and “his name” is his gospel, “which Isaiah calls his law” — they who “wait for it” truly “hope, or trust in it.” Clarke renders “they shall hope,” Christ being “the sole hope… of mankind”; the prophecy “began to have its accomplishment in the Idumeans, Tyrians, and Sidonians” then attending him.

The Puritan Henry draws out “the great design of the gospel… to bring people to trust in the name of Jesus Christ” — “The Lord our Righteousness.” He joins it to the promise that “God shall persuade Japhet to dwell in the tents of Shem” (Genesis 9:27), now fulfilled when the Gentiles “wait for his law, and trust in his name.” They “only… can with confidence trust in Christ’s name, that wait for his law with a resolution to be ruled by it” — the law we wait for being “the law of faith… that we believe in Christ” (1 John 3:23). The Reformed Calvin sums the whole: though the words differ from the prophet’s, “the meaning is the same, that the grace of Christ will be shared by the Gentiles.”

The witnesses make the verse the joyful goal of the prophecy. After the Servant’s gentleness comes his reach: “in his name shall the Gentiles trust” — the calling of the nations once “hid from any place” now thrown open. Henry binds it to the ancient promise that “God shall persuade Japhet to dwell in the tents of Shem” (Genesis 9:27), fulfilled when the isles “wait for his law, and trust in his name.” The trust required is no idle confidence: they only “can with confidence trust in Christ’s name, that wait for his law with a resolution to be ruled by it,” the law of faith being “that we believe in Christ” (1 John 3:23). Calvin sums the whole prophecy: though Matthew’s words differ from the prophet’s, “the meaning is the same, that the grace of Christ will be shared by the Gentiles” — the despised flock of the lowly Servant gathered from all the earth.
22:
On Matthew 12:22 — “then was brought unto him one possessed with a devil, blind, and dumb: and he healed him” — the witnesses note the threefold cure and its occasion for the great blasphemy.

The Latin and eastern fathers dwell on the miracle. Jerome marks “three miracles… wrought in one… person at the same time; the blind sees, the dumb speaks, the possessed is delivered,” now “daily fulfilled in the conversion of them that believe.” Chrysostom wonders at the demon’s malice in having “obstructed both inlets by which he could believe… hearing and sight,” which Christ “opened.” Hilary and Augustine read the man as a figure of the Gentile or the unbeliever — “he that believes not, is truly demoniac, blind, and dumb… subject to the devil” — made now “meet to receive God.”

The Puritan Henry draws the spiritual sense. The man’s case was “sad”: blind and dumb, he “could neither see to help himself, nor speak to others.” So “a soul under Satan’s power… is blind in the things of God, and dumb at the throne of grace” — “Satan blinds the eye of faith, and seals up the lips of prayer.” The cure shows that “the conquering and dispossessing of Satan is the healing of souls”: when his power is broken, “the eyes are opened to see God’s glory, and the lips opened to speak his praise.” Gill notes the parallel and distinct case of Matthew 9:32, and the Gloss that the Evangelist sets the miracle first because the Pharisees “perversely attributed” it “to an unclean spirit.”

The witnesses read the cure as both wonder and warfare. Christ came, Henry notes, “to destroy the works of the devil,” and “more cases of this kind occurred in Christ’s time than usual, that… his power might be the more magnified” in “dispossessing Satan.” The man’s double affliction is read as the soul’s estate under sin: “blind” in “the things of God,” “dumb at the throne of grace,” for “Satan blinds the eye of faith, and seals up the lips of prayer.” Jerome makes it the pattern of every conversion — “the daemon is cast out when they first behold the light of the faith, and then their mouths… are opened to utter the praises of God.” The miracle stands first in the narrative, the Gloss observes, because it was the very work the Pharisees, “with a yet greater wickedness,” ascribed to “an unclean spirit” — the occasion of the great blasphemy that follows.
23:
On Matthew 12:23 — “and all the people were amazed, and said, Is not this the Son of David?” — the witnesses note the people’s wondering confession.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators read the question two ways. Henry takes it first “as an enquiring question” — they asked but “did not stay for an answer”; the impressions were “cogent, but… transient” — and then “as an affirming question,” the miracle plainly evincing “that the kingdom of the Messiah is now setting up.” “Son of David” was the familiar title of the promised Messiah; Gill notes the cure had “the same effect upon the people” as that of Matthew 9:33.

Henry marks who drew the inference: “the people, the vulgar sort,” not the learned, “because they were less prejudiced… by worldly interest.” So “plain and easy was the way made to this great truth of Christ being the Messiah… that the common people could not miss it” (Isaiah 35:8) — “the wayfaring men, though fools, could not err therein.” It is “an instance of the condescensions of divine grace, that the things… hid from the wise and prudent were revealed unto babes” (1 Corinthians 1:27). The wonder of the simple, all the witnesses agree, is set deliberately against the blasphemy of the rulers that immediately follows.

The witnesses note that the people’s wonder was the seed of a true confession, soon stifled. Henry takes the question first as “enquiring” — cogent impressions that, not pursued, proved “transient” — warning that “such convictions… should be brought to a head, and then they are likely to be brought to the heart.” Read as “affirming,” it owns that “the kingdom of the Messiah is now setting up.” That it was “the people, the vulgar sort,” who drew the inference, while the learned Pharisees blasphemed, is to Henry a signal mercy: they were “less prejudiced… by worldly interest,” so “the wayfaring men, though fools, could not err therein” (Isaiah 35:8). It is, he concludes, “an instance of the condescensions of divine grace, that the things… hid from the wise and prudent were revealed unto babes” (1 Corinthians 1:27) — the wonder of the simple framing the rulers’ guilt.
24:
On Matthew 12:24 — “but when the Pharisees heard it, they said, This fellow doth not cast out devils, but by Beelzebub the prince of the devils” — the witnesses expose the blasphemous cavil.

The Latin fathers explain the name. Remigius and Rabanus derive Beelzebub from Baal — “Zebub means a fly,” so “Beelzebub… the man of flies,” the god of Ekron — “having nothing more mean to cast upon the Lord, they said that He cast out daemons by Beelzebub.” Rabanus marks the contrast: the multitude “wondered at the works,” but the Pharisees “laboured to pervert by an ill interpretation” what they could not deny, ascribing it “to an unclean spirit.”

The Puritan Henry lays bare the malice. The Pharisees, “irritated” at the people’s cry, envied Christ “as Saul did… David” (1 Samuel 18:8); “the more people magnified Christ, the more industrious they were to vilify him.” They speak scornfully — “this fellow” — and blasphemously, being “forced to grant the premises” (the miracle was “as plain as the sun”) yet evading the conclusion by “suggesting… a compact between Christ and the devil.” “No surmise could be more… false and vile… that he, who is Truth itself, should be in combination with the father of lies” — “the last refuge… of an obstinate infidelity.” The grammatical-historical voices add that “Beelzebul” is the right form (JFB), the name of “the prince of the devils,” “the ringleader of the apostasy.”

The witnesses trace the cavil to its root in envy. The Pharisees, Henry notes, were “proud of the reputation they had among the people”; when they heard the cry “Is not this the Son of David?” they were irritated “more at that than at the miracle itself,” fearing their own interest “eclipsed” — envying Christ “as Saul did… David” (1 Samuel 18:8). “Those who bind up their happiness in the praise… of men expose themselves to a perpetual uneasiness” at any good word of another. Forced to grant the miracle — “as plain as the sun” — they took “the last refuge… of an obstinate infidelity,” alleging “a compact between Christ and the devil.” The very name they used, the fathers note, was the meanest they could find — Beelzebub, lord of flies, a fallen Lucifer made “a lord of flies” — flung at him who is “Truth itself” to brand him a confederate of “the father of lies.”
25:
On Matthew 12:25 — “Jesus knew their thoughts, and said unto them, Every kingdom divided against itself is brought to desolation” — the witnesses note Christ’s knowledge of the heart and the first argument of his reply.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators mark his omniscience. Henry: “Jesus knew their thoughts… knows what is in man; he understands our thoughts afar off.” The Pharisees, it seems, “could not for shame speak it out, but kept it in their minds” — reserving the blasphemy “for the silencing of the convictions” of the people — yet Christ answered the unspoken thought. Barnes and the others note the appeal to a self-evident maxim of politics: “every kingdom divided against itself” — whether a nation, a city, or a house — “is brought to desolation,” for internal division is ruin.

The argument, the witnesses agree, is one from common reason that the Pharisees could not gainsay. Civil war destroys a kingdom; faction destroys a house. To suppose that Satan would lend his power to cast out his own servants is to suppose his kingdom armed against itself — a self-defeating absurdity. Christ thus meets the slander not with mere denial but with a reasoned exposure of its folly, drawing his proof, as so often, from things “within the reach of their senses,” that the malice of the charge might be plain to all who heard. The principle laid down — that division spells desolation — will return in the chapter’s warning of the house left empty and the unclean spirit’s return.

The witnesses ground the reply in Christ’s knowledge of the heart. He “knew their thoughts,” Henry notes — for “Jesus Christ knows what we are thinking… he understands our thoughts afar off” — answering a blasphemy the Pharisees “could not for shame speak out, but kept… in their minds.” The argument he draws is “from common reason,” a maxim no man could deny: division is the ruin of every society, “every kingdom… city… house” so divided “brought to desolation.” By it Christ exposes the folly of the charge without mere denial — civil war destroys a realm, faction a family — drawing his proof, as so often, from things “within the reach of their senses.” The principle laid down, the witnesses note, runs on to the chapter’s close, where the empty house and the returning spirit show a soul, and a nation, undone by its own inward want of unity with God.
26:
On Matthew 12:26 — “and if Satan cast out Satan, he is divided against himself; how shall then his kingdom stand?” — the witnesses read the application of the maxim to the charge.

The grammatical-historical commentators draw out the reductio. If, as the Pharisees alleged, Christ cast out devils “by Beelzebub the prince of the devils,” then Satan was “divided against himself” — the prince undoing the work of his own subjects. But a kingdom so divided “cannot stand”; the supposition therefore destroys itself. The casting out of devils, far from proving a league with Satan, proves the very opposite — the breaking-in of a stronger power upon his realm. Gill and the others note the absurdity: Satan “is not divided against himself,” his kingdom being held together by a fierce unity of malice against God and man.

The witnesses observe that Christ here turns the slander back upon the slanderers. The Pharisees acknowledged that devils were cast out; they only disputed by whose power. By their own admission, then, a kingdom was being overthrown — and since Satan does not war against himself, the power at work must be divine. The argument prepares for the plainer word that follows (Matthew 12:28): that if Christ casts out devils “by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God is come unto you.” What the Pharisees meant as the “last refuge” of unbelief is shown to be, on its own terms, the surest evidence that the Messiah and his kingdom had arrived — Satan’s house being entered and his goods spoiled.

The witnesses make the verse a self-destroying supposition. If Christ cast out devils by Satan’s power, then Satan was set against his own subjects, his kingdom armed against itself — and so “cannot stand.” The casting out of devils, far from proving a league, proves “a stronger power” breaking in upon his realm. Satan, the witnesses note, “is not divided against himself,” his kingdom held by “a fierce unity of malice against God and man”; so the Pharisees’ own admission — that devils were indeed cast out — convicts them. The argument prepares for the plain word that follows: that if it be “by the Spirit of God,” then “the kingdom of God is come unto you” (Matthew 12:28). What the rulers meant as the last refuge of unbelief is shown to be, on its own terms, the surest evidence that the Messiah had come, Satan’s house being entered and his goods spoiled.
27:
On Matthew 12:27 — “and if I by Beelzebub cast out devils, by whom do your children cast them out? therefore they shall be your judges” — the witnesses read the second answer, an argument from the Pharisees’ own.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify “your children” as the Jewish exorcists or disciples of the Pharisees (compare the sons of Sceva, Acts 19:13). Barnes states the force: “if your argument be true… that a man who casts out devils must be in league with the devil, then your disciples have made a covenant with him also” — so they must “either give up this argument, or admit that the working of miracles is proof of the assistance of God.” Clarke calls it “extremely conclusive,” turning the charge back: “envy causes persons often to condemn in one, what they approve in another.” Ellicott notes the logic “requires… the admission that the children… did really cast out demons, and that not by Beelzebub.”

The Latin and eastern fathers draw out the “judges.” Chrysostom notes Christ said “your children,” not “my disciples,” that they “might take occasion” to return, yet have “no impudent excuse”; Jerome: “they shall be your judges, not by authority but by comparison,” ascribing such works to God while the Pharisees ascribe Christ’s to the prince of devils. The Reformed Calvin reads it as charging them with “an unjust and malicious decision,” condemning in Christ “what they praised in their own” — the objection being “of a personal nature,” aimed not at the exorcists’ merits but at the Pharisees’ partiality.

The witnesses note the wisdom of arguing from the Pharisees’ own exorcists. By “your children” Christ named, not his disciples, but those of the accusers’ party — so that, if the children’s casting out of devils was ascribed “to God, and not to daemons,” the same work in Christ must “have the same cause” (Jerome). The charge, Calvin observes, was “of a personal nature”: Christ does not pause to vindicate the exorcists’ standing, but exposes the “inequality” that “condemned in Christ what they praised in their own.” This “blind love or hatred” was the very mark of malice. Chrysostom adds the door left open — he said “your children” that, “if they chose to return… they might take occasion hence,” but “if they should be ungrateful, they might not have even an impudent excuse.” They are made “your judges,” the witnesses agree, by the comparison of their own consistent practice.
28:
On Matthew 12:28 — “but if I cast out devils by the Spirit of God, then the kingdom of God is come unto you” — the witnesses read the conclusion of the argument and its announcement of the kingdom.

The grammatical-historical commentators set out the reasoning. Ellicott formulates it: “the work was confessedly superhuman, either from the power of Satan or that of God,” the former excluded by the previous verses, “the latter… therefore the only explanation” — whence Jesus “was what He claimed to be, the Head of the divine kingdom.” Luke’s parallel has “the finger of God” (Luke 11:20; Exodus 8:19), the Spirit being the power by which Christ worked. Barnes: “if God expels Satan… then his reign has come” (Daniel 2:44); Clarke notes the word “is come” means “come unexpectedly upon you.”

The eastern Chrysostom hears in it both a proof and an appeal: casting out devils is “a matter of great power… not an ordinary grace,” and the words say in effect, “if this indeed be so, then is the Son of God come upon you” — “these good things are coming for you; why do you oppose your own salvation?” The Reformed Calvin presses the same complaint of ingratitude: Christ “treats them as convicted persons,” reminding them that God, “by revealing His Messiah,” was about “to restore his kingdom among them,” while they “do everything… to drive him away.” The misattached Cyril fragment on the Spirit’s varied operations sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses press the verse as the turning of defence into summons. Having silenced the slander, Christ “treats them as convicted persons” (Calvin), charging them “not to make ungodly opposition to the kingdom of God.” He does not rest in the single miracle but takes from it “the object of his coming” — that God, “by revealing His Messiah,” was come to “raise up their salvation which was fallen.” The word “is come upon you,” Chrysostom notes, says in effect “these good things are coming for you; why do you oppose your own salvation?” That casting out devils is “a matter of great power, and not an ordinary grace” makes it “the very sign given by the Prophets of the presence of the Son of God.” So the kingdom they professed to await had “burst upon men unawares” (Ellicott), and their unbelief was the more inexcusable for rejecting what their own desire had long sought.
29:
On Matthew 12:29 — “or else how can one enter into a strong man’s house, and spoil his goods, except he first bind the strong man?” — the witnesses read the third answer, the parable of the spoiling.

The fathers expound the figure. Chrysostom: Christ proves “the very contrary of that they strove to establish” — “so far am I from having the Devil for my ally, that I rather am at war with him, and bind him”; the binding of “the prince of the daemons” is shown by the spoiling of his subjects. Jerome: “his house is this world… the strong man is bound… bruised by the Lord’s foot.” Augustine: “by his goods, he means all the unbelievers,” the binding being that Christ “has taken away from him all power of hindering the faithful.” Rabanus: Christ “by the might of His divinity frees men from the daemons.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators read it the same. Ellicott identifies the “strong man” as Satan, the “house” the region he rules, the “goods” the demons, the “binding” the check given his tyranny, the “spoiling” the “final victory” (Luke 11:22). Calvin draws the necessity of redemption: Satan, “a strong and powerful tyrant,” will “never yield of his own accord,” and “it is in vain for men to expect deliverance, till Satan has been subdued by a violent struggle” — the scribes betraying “ignorance… of the kingdom of God” in resisting its beginnings. The misattached Cyril fragment, marshalling the verse against dualist heretics, sits beside it as a stray.

The witnesses make the parable a window on redemption itself. Barnes draws the figure: “a man could not break into the house of a strong man… unless he had rendered the man himself helpless”; so Christ, taking the possessed from “the dominion of Satan,” gives “clear proof that I have subdued Satan himself.” Calvin presses the analogy between the visible and spiritual cures (Matthew 8:17): in rescuing men’s senses from the devil, Christ proclaimed himself sent “to destroy his spiritual tyranny over their souls.” The tyrant “will never yield of his own accord,” held in “quiet possession” since Adam “subjected all his posterity to this foreign sway”; deliverance is therefore “in vain… till Satan has been subdued by a violent struggle.” Most men, Calvin laments, “loudly boast that they desire the kingdom of God,” yet, like a sick man refusing the physician’s remedy, “do not permit Christ to fight… to rescue us.”
30:
On Matthew 12:30 — “he that is not with me is against me; and he that gathereth not with me scattereth abroad” — the witnesses read the fourth answer, drawing the line of cleavage.

The grammatical-historical commentators apply it first to Satan and to the Pharisees. Barnes: “there could be but two parties… as I have not united with Satan, but opposed him, there can be no league between us.” Robertson: “Jesus draws the line of cleavage between himself and his enemies… we either gather with Christ or scatter… Christ is the magnet of the ages.” Ellicott reconciles it with “he that is not against us is for us” (Luke 9:50): the two are “the opposite poles of the same truth,” for “in the great struggle… there is no neutrality” — the man casting out devils in Christ’s name was with him, the slandering Pharisees on the side of Satan.

The fathers and Reformed read it of Christ’s opposition to the devil. Jerome: it refers “to the Devil… in that the works of the Saviour cannot be compared with the works of Beelzebub” — the one would “hold men’s souls in captivity,” the other “set them free.” Calvin allows both senses but leans to the charge against the scribes: they were “in two respects opposed to the kingdom” — failing to assist it, and even raging openly against it — and adds the wider lesson that “they are unworthy to be considered as belonging to the flock of Christ, who do not apply… all the means” in their power. Wesley sums it: “there are no neuters in this war.”

The witnesses draw out the searching personal force of the line. There being “no neuters in this war” (Wesley), “every one must be either with Christ or against him; either a loyal subject or a rebel.” Clarke turns it inward: “in vain do men seek for methods to reconcile God and mammon… there is no medium between… loving the Lord and being his enemy”; and when “Christ, his truth, and his servants are assaulted, he who does not espouse their cause… incurs the guilt of deserting… him.” Calvin widens it to a standing duty: “whoever does not assist is, in some measure, opposed” to Christ, so that “they are unworthy to be considered as belonging to the flock of Christ, who do not apply to it all the means that are in their power.” The image of gathering and scattering, Robertson notes, makes Christ “the magnet of the ages” — “he draws or drives away.”
31:
On Matthew 12:31 — “all manner of sin and blasphemy shall be forgiven unto men: but the blasphemy against the Holy Ghost shall not be forgiven” — the witnesses divide over the nature of the unpardonable sin.

The grammatical-historical and most patristic voices fix it on the specific blasphemy before them. Barnes and Clarke, with Mark’s explanation “because they said, He hath an unclean spirit” (Mark 3:30), define it as “ascribing the miracles Christ wrought, by the power of God, to the spirit of the devil” — “the highest displays of God’s mercy… alleged… the work of the devil.” Chrysostom: they might plead ignorance of Christ “because of the flesh,” but not of the Spirit, by whom the prophets spoke; Jerome: he who, “unable to deny the power of God, speaks falsely against” it “prompted by jealousy,” finds no pardon. Clarke comforts the anxious: “no man who believes the Divine mission of Jesus Christ ever can commit this sin.”

The Latin father Augustine, in his long treatment, reads it rather as final impenitence: “impenitence itself… is the blasphemy against the Spirit” — the “hard and impenitent heart” resisting “the gift of free grace,” the remission of sins given in the Spirit by whom “the Church is gathered into one” — so that “we must despair of none so long as the patience of God leads to repentance.” The witnesses add the guardrails: forgiveness is promised, says Remigius, only to such as “have performed due penitence” (against Novatian); the Gloss notes the verse “destroys that heresy of Origen” of universal pardon; and Gregory infers from “neither in this world, neither in that to come” that “some sins… are remitted in the world to come,” the lesser faults of those who “merited the same in this life.”

The witnesses, however they define the sin, agree on its dreadfulness and its limits. Barnes fixes it as “a wanton and blasphemous attack on the Divine power and nature of Christ… very different from what is now often supposed”; the anxious, Clarke comforts, need not fear, for “no man who believes the Divine mission of Jesus Christ ever can commit this sin.” Augustine’s long inquiry reaches the same practical end — that “we must despair of none so long as the patience of God leads to repentance,” since “place for penitence… was never denied in the Church.” The guardrails the fathers add fence the doctrine on both sides: against Novatian’s denial of pardon to the lapsed (Remigius), against Origen’s dream of universal forgiveness (the Gloss). The phrase “neither in this world, nor in that to come” means, all the grammatical-historical voices agree, simply “hath never forgiveness” — not that any sin is forgiven in hell.
32:
On Matthew 12:32 — “whosoever speaketh a word against the Son of man, it shall be forgiven him: but whosoever speaketh against the Holy Ghost, it shall not be forgiven” — the witnesses draw the distinction between the two blasphemies.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain the contrast. Barnes: “the Jews were offended at the humble life” of Jesus — reproaching him as a Nazarene, a Galilean — and “reflections of this kind could be pardoned”; but to reproach “his Divine character, charging it as being in league with Satan,… attributing… divinity to the prince of fallen spirits, there could be no pardon.” “Neither in this world, neither in the world to come” means, as Mark has it, “hath never forgiveness” (Mark 3:29) — “the guilt will be unpardoned for ever” — not that “any sins will be forgiven in hell.”

The Latin fathers draw the same line with their own emphases. Jerome: to speak against the Son of man “as stumbling at My flesh, and thinking of Me as no more than man… finds pardon because of the little worth of the body”; but to call “the works of the Holy Ghost… Beelzebub” is not forgiven. Augustine warns the phrase “the word” (not “any word”) means “some word” — not every utterance against the Spirit is unpardonable, since Pagans, Jews, and heretics who once blasphemed the Spirit yet find forgiveness on conversion; the irremissible thing is the impenitence that “resists the gift of the Holy Spirit” in which “remission of sin” is conveyed. The misattached Cyril fragment on the one Spirit’s operations sits beside the verse as a stray.

The witnesses dwell on the mercy hidden in the harder word. That a word against the Son of man may be forgiven means, Barnes notes, that “reflections… on his poverty, his humble birth” — the stumbling of those who saw only the Nazarene — “might be forgiven” on repentance. Augustine even reads the Pharisees’ present blasphemy as itself pardonable, “if they should be converted, and believe”; the irremissible thing is the “impenitent heart” that “treasures up… wrath against the day of wrath.” The distinction, then, is not that the Spirit is greater than the Son — “which no heretic ever affirmed” — but that to resist the Spirit’s gift of remission, persisted in to the end, leaves no avenue of pardon. The warning, the witnesses agree, is severe precisely to drive men to the grace they are tempted to despise: as long as life lasts, “we must despair of none.”
33:
On Matthew 12:33 — “either make the tree good, and his fruit good; or else make the tree corrupt, and his fruit corrupt: for the tree is known by his fruit” — the witnesses read the figure as exposing the Pharisees’ corrupt words.

The grammatical-historical commentators take the maxim as a test of consistency. The principle — “the tree is known by his fruit” — means, all agree, that the words and works of a man reveal his nature: the Pharisees’ venomous speech betrayed a corrupt heart, just as good fruit proves a good tree. Christ presses them to be consistent: either own his works good and confess the tree good, or, calling the fruit (his miracles) corrupt, stand convicted of the absurdity of a good tree bearing evil fruit. Wesley sums it: “the tree is known by its fruit — namely, the heart by the conversation.”

The Reformed Calvin draws out the application to speech. The likeness of tree and fruit “is here applied… to nothing more than speech, because this afforded an opportunity of detecting the inward and concealed malice of the scribes”: their “falsehood and slanders betrayed what was not so visible in the rest of their life.” Christ “waxes wroth” against those whom “ambition, or envy… prompts to slander,” for the passage “shows how highly valuable in the sight of the Lord truth is.” He cautions that proverbs “state nothing more than the ordinary fact” — sometimes a wicked man feigns “almost angelical purity of language” — yet “it is an invariable truth, that bad speeches are indications of a bad heart,” the tongue being “the index of the mind.”

The witnesses make the tree and fruit a summons to honest self-knowledge. Christ presses the Pharisees to consistency: a good tree cannot bear evil fruit, nor a corrupt tree good; their venomous words therefore betray a corrupt heart. The principle reaches every man — “the heart by the conversation” (Wesley) — for words and works are the fruit by which the hidden root is known. Calvin, applying it to speech, notes that the slander “detected the inward and concealed malice of the scribes,” and that the passage “shows how highly valuable in the sight of the Lord truth is.” He cautions that the proverb states “the ordinary fact” only — a cunning man may feign “the purity of an Angel” — yet holds it “an invariable truth, that bad speeches are indications of a bad heart.” The Lord, the witnesses conclude, “extorts from each… some kind of confession,” so that men “discover by the tongue their natural disposition.”
34:
On Matthew 12:34 — “O generation of vipers, how can ye, being evil, speak good things? for out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh” — the witnesses read the sharp exposure of the source of speech.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark the severity and its ground. Christ addresses them as “a generation of vipers” — a brood venomous by nature — and asks how the evil can “speak good things,” since speech flows “out of the abundance of the heart.” The word, all agree, is the overflow of the inward store: an evil heart, however it may disguise itself, betrays itself at last by the tongue. Wesley notes “the heart… by the conversation” is known.
