The Reformed Calvin presses why Christ uses such harshness. “There is no reason to wonder,” Christ says in effect, “that you vomit out wicked words; for your heart is full of malice.” Other sins call for milder reproof, “but when hypocritical persons pervert what is right… such wickedness renders it necessary that God should thunder against it.” The Pharisees were “so captivated by an unfounded conviction of their righteousness, that an ordinary warning had no effect”; “till hypocrites are sharply pierced… all that is said to them is treated with scorn.” The lesson the witnesses draw is that the heart is the fountain: “whatever hidden and crooked recesses may exist in the heart… the Lord extorts from each… some kind of confession,” so that men “discover by the tongue their natural disposition.”

The witnesses feel the weight of so sharp a word from so gentle a Master. The reproach “generation of vipers” is, Calvin explains, no excess but a holy necessity: where “hypocritical persons pervert what is right, or put a false coloring on what is sinful, such wickedness renders it necessary that God should thunder against it in a more terrible manner than against other sins.” The Pharisees were “so captivated by an unfounded conviction of their righteousness” that “an ordinary warning had no effect” — “till hypocrites are sharply pierced… all that is said to them is treated with scorn.” The verse fixes the source of all speech in the heart: “out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh.” However men “conceal their vices,” the witnesses conclude, the tongue at last betrays the inward store — the evil heart no more able to “speak good things” than the viper to change its venom.
35:
On Matthew 12:35 — “a good man out of the good treasure of the heart bringeth forth good things: and an evil man out of the evil treasure bringeth forth evil things” — the witnesses read the heart as the storehouse of speech.

The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the “treasure.” Ellicott calls the word “a whole parable”: “every thought and desire… is added to the ever-accumulating store… in the inner chamber of his heart, and thence passes out into word or deed.” Where there is not “the unity of goodness which comes from the love of God, there must be the distraction… that comes from the love of self,” making the treasure “predominantly evil” — “the poison of worldliness acts… with accumulative power” (compare Matthew 13:52). Clarke: “the good heart is the good treasury, and the treasure… is the love of God, and of all mankind”; the bad heart’s treasure is “the carnal mind, which is enmity against God.”

The Reformed Gill expounds “the good treasure” as the grace of God implanted: not what a man is “naturally possessed of, but what is put into him” — “that grace for grace, which he has received out of Christ’s fulness,” a treasure of “various graces, each… more precious than gold.” The verse thus completes the argument of the tree and its fruit: as the good man’s renewed heart yields good things, so the Pharisees’ blasphemy issued from “the evil treasure” within. The speech is no accident but the steady outflow of the heart’s hidden store, and so the surest index of what a man truly is.

The witnesses complete the figure of the heart as a storehouse. Every word and deed, Ellicott notes, is drawn from “the ever-accumulating store… in the inner chamber of his heart”; where the love of God does not reign, “the love of self” makes the treasure “predominantly evil,” “the poison of worldliness” acting “with accumulative power.” Gill presses that the good treasure is no native possession but grace “put into him” — “that grace for grace, which he has received out of Christ’s fulness,” a hoard of “various graces, each… more precious than gold.” The verse thus seals the argument against the Pharisees: their blasphemy was no accident of the tongue but the steady outflow of “the evil treasure” within. As the good man’s renewed heart yields good, so theirs yielded slander — speech being, on every reading, “the index of the mind,” the truest measure of what a man is.
36:
On Matthew 12:36 — “every idle word that men shall speak, they shall give account thereof in the day of judgment” — the witnesses weigh the solemn reckoning for speech.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the “idle word.” Clarke takes argon as “a word that does nothing,” answering to the Hebrew for what is “vain… wicked and injurious,” and notes the scope “condemning all false and injurious words.” Ellicott observes Christ says not “every evil word” but “every idle… word,” the “random utterances which… betray character more than deliberate speech” — such as the taunt “He casteth out devils by Beelzebub” — and it is not that for each a man is condemned, but that he must “give an account,” it entering into “that great total which determines the divine estimate of his character.” Robertson: “our words reveal our thoughts and form a just basis for the interpretation of character” — “judgment by words” as Matthew 25:31–46 is judgment by deeds.

The eastern Chrysostom turns it to comfort the accused: “the Judge will pass sentence… according to what you have yourself spoken,” so “they that are accused have no need to fear, but they that accuse.” The Reformed Calvin reads it as “an argument from the less to the greater”: if idle words are called in question, “how would God spare the open blasphemies” of those who “bark against his glory”? Yet, lest it drive us to despair, he adds that as God “will bury in gracious forgetfulness the sins which deserve… deaths,” so “he will likewise pardon the guilt of idle talking” — the threat being a call to govern the tongue (Ephesians 5:4; Colossians 4:6).

The witnesses press the solemnity of the reckoning for speech. That even an idle word — “an ineffective, useless word… that does no good and so is pernicious” (Robertson) — must be accounted for shows how Christ, who “knows our very thoughts,” insists that “our words reveal our thoughts.” Calvin reasons “from the less to the greater”: if idle talk is questioned, “how would God spare the open blasphemies… of those who bark against his glory?” Yet he will not leave us in despair: as God “will bury in gracious forgetfulness the sins which deserve… deaths… he will likewise pardon the guilt of idle talking” — the threat being a call to govern the tongue, to “speak of the sacred mysteries… with the utmost reverence,” and to “abstain from talkativeness, buffoonery, and… slanderous attacks” (Colossians 4:6). Chrysostom turns it to the comfort of the accused, for the Judge sentences “according to what you have yourself spoken.”
37:
On Matthew 12:37 — “for by thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned” — the witnesses read words as the evidence of the heart at the judgment.

The grammatical-historical commentators take it as a proverbial principle of trial. Barnes: “words shall be the indication of the true principles of the heart… as the tree is by its fruit” — not “the only thing” for which a man is condemned, “but… an important part” (James 3:6). Clarke: “the whole tenor of thy conversation will be an evidence for or against thee” — “how many count words for nothing! and yet eternity often depends on them.” Ellicott, stripping off “the after-thoughts… of theologians,” takes “justified” as simply “the opposite of condemned”: men are justified by faith, by works, and — as here — by words, all being “elements of a man’s character.”

The Reformed Calvin meets the Papists’ attempt to wield the verse against justification by faith. It is “childish”: a man is not justified “because his speech is the ground of his justification,” but because “pure… speech absolves us, so that we are not condemned… by our tongue.” The contrast of the two words rather upholds the right sense of “justify” — “to be accounted righteous… before the tribunal of God.” Wesley sums it: “your words as well as actions shall be produced in evidence… to prove whether you was a true believer or not.”

The witnesses take the verse as the principle that closes the whole address. Words, Barnes notes, “will be an important part of the things for which” a man is judged (James 3:6) — they who “count words for nothing” forget that “eternity often depends on them” (Clarke). Ellicott sets “justified” in its plain sense, “the opposite of condemned,” and harmonizes the three biblical justifications — by faith, by works, by words — as “alike elements of a man’s character”: faith its root, words its “most spontaneous manifestation,” works its “more permanent results.” Calvin meets the abuse of the text against justification by faith: it is “childish,” for speech is not “the ground of” justification but the evidence by which a man is “not condemned… by his tongue” — the very contrast of the two words confirming that to justify is “to be accounted righteous… before the tribunal of God.”
38:
On Matthew 12:38 — “Master, we would see a sign from thee” — the witnesses note the hypocrisy of the demand after so many signs.

The eastern Chrysostom marks the irony: “now they were railing against Him… now they fawn upon Him, calling Him, Master,” that “they may take Him as in a snare” — and the Evangelist wonders that “when they should have been… dumb with astonishment,” they “desist not from their malice.” Jerome notes they sought “a sign from heaven” — fire like Elijah’s, or thunder out of season — “as though they could not have spoken falsely even against such miracles.” Rabanus: he gives them a sign “not from heaven, which they were unworthy to see, but… from the deep beneath.”

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators expose the inconsistency. Gill notes they now address him not as “fellow” or demoniac but flatteringly as “Master,” willing “to divert the discourse.” Henry: it was “highly reasonable that they should see a sign” that he might prove his mission “by miracles,” but “highly unreasonable to demand a sign now, when he had given so many.” “It is natural to proud men to prescribe to God, and then to make that an excuse for not subscribing to him; but a man’s offence will never be his defence.” The Pharisees who complimented him with “Master” pretended respect “when they intended to abuse him” — for “all are not indeed Christ’s servants, who call him Master.”

The witnesses mark the malice beneath the courteous request. The Pharisees who now say “Master” had just called him a confederate of Beelzebub; their flattery, Chrysostom notes, is as much a snare as their railing, that “they may take Him” — and Christ, “above either affection,” was “neither moved to anger by evil speaking, nor… gained by flattery.” Henry presses the unreasonableness: a sign to prove his mission was fitting, but to “demand a sign now, when he had given so many,” was the device of proud men who “prescribe to God, and then… make that an excuse for not subscribing to him” — “but a man’s offence will never be his defence.” The title they gave him only deepened their guilt: “all are not indeed Christ’s servants, who call him Master.” They sought “a sign from heaven” (Jerome) precisely because they were resolved to believe none.
39:
On Matthew 12:39 — “an evil and adulterous generation seeketh after a sign; and there shall no sign be given to it, but the sign of the prophet Jonas” — the witnesses explain the rebuke and the one sign reserved.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound “adulterous.” The covenant of Israel with God was figured as marriage (Isaiah 57:3; Hosea 3:1), so their unfaithfulness is “adultery” (Barnes, Ellicott) — Clarke adds there was “the utmost proof… that… they were most literally an adulterous race.” Clarke renders the verb “seeketh another sign,” demanding “sign upon sign” after so many given. The sign of Jonas, Barnes explains, is the resurrection — “as Jonah was preserved three days… so He would be raised from the dead after three days” — an evidence as decisive as Jonah’s preservation was to Nineveh.

The Reformed Calvin presses that the rebuke is not merely for asking a sign (Gideon and Hezekiah asked and were not blamed) but for “malice” — they were “ungrateful to God,” despising “so many of his wonderful works,” and sought “a subterfuge for not obeying his word.” He marks the single point of the Jonah likeness: “he will be their prophet after that he is risen from the dead” — “as Jonah came from the bottom of the sea to Nineveh.” Henry reads them “an adulterous brood… degenerated from… their ancestors” and “an adulterous wife,” departed from God; Wesley: “such adulterers are all those who love the world.”

The witnesses dwell on the justice of giving no sign but Jonah’s. Christ, Henry notes, “is always ready to hear… holy desires… yet he will not gratify corrupt lusts”: signs were granted “to those who desired them for the confirmation of their faith, as to Abraham and Gideon,” but “denied to those who demanded them for the excuse of their unbelief.” Justly might he have said “they shall never see another miracle”; instead, in “wonderful goodness,” he leaves them the signs already given and adds one more — the resurrection. Calvin marks the single point of the Jonah likeness: as Jonah, “brought up from the bottom of the sea, preached to the Ninevites,” so Christ “will be their prophet after that he is risen from the dead.” The unbelief that would “shift off” even that sign — saying “his disciples… stole him away” — proves, Henry adds, that “none are so incurably blind as those who are resolved they will not see.”
40:
On Matthew 12:40 — “as Jonas was three days and three nights in the whale’s belly; so shall the Son of man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth” — the witnesses treat the sign and the reckoning of the days.

The grammatical-historical commentators settle the “three days and three nights.” Christ was in the grave “but two nights and a part of three days” (Barnes), yet this is “strictly in accordance with the Jewish mode of reckoning,” by which “a part of a day was… received as the whole” (so Lightfoot at length, citing the rabbinic onah; compare Esther 4:16 with 5:1; Genesis 42:17–18); had it been otherwise, the Jews “would have charged… as a false prophet.” The “whale” (Greek kētos) is “a great fish… a sea-monster,” likely a shark (Gill, Clarke, Robertson). “The heart of the earth” means the grave (Barnes), and, parallel to Jonah’s “belly of hell” (Jonah 2:2), implies for Ellicott “the descent into Hades.”

Ellicott defends the verse against those who deem it a later insertion: Jonah’s “sign” was nothing in his history “except the… whale”; the very chronological difficulty argues against a prophecy written after the event; and since Christ elsewhere foretold his resurrection (John 2:19; Matthew 16:21), “the history of Jonah presented an analogy which it was natural that He should notice.” Henry draws the parallels: the grave was to Christ “as the belly of the fish was to Jonah,” both prisoners — Jonah for his own sins, Christ “for ours” — and both returning “to the land of the living.” The misattached Cyril fragment, drawing out the Jonah resemblance, sits beside the verse.

The witnesses take pains over the reckoning, both for honesty and for comfort. The “three days and three nights” were not three full days, all agree, but, by the settled Jewish idiom, “a part… received as the whole” — Christ “buried in the afternoon of the sixth day… and rose… in the morning of the first” (Henry). That the Jews never charged him with falsehood on this point, Barnes notes, “is plain… that what was meant… was accomplished.” Henry draws the parallels of grace: as Jonah in the fish’s belly “comforted himself” that he should “look again toward God’s holy temple” (Jonah 2:4), so Christ “in the grave is… said to rest in hope” (Acts 2:26), assured he should “not see corruption”; and as Jonah on the third day “came to the land of the living,” so Christ should “rise out of his grave to send abroad the gospel to the Gentiles.”
41:
On Matthew 12:41 — “the men of Nineveh shall rise in judgment with this generation, and shall condemn it… and, behold, a greater than Jonas is here” — the witnesses read the heathen city’s repentance as Israel’s shame.

The grammatical-historical commentators explain “rise” and “condemn.” The word “rise” is used “not of the mere fact of resurrection but of standing up as witnesses” (Ellicott); they condemn “not by a sentence of judgment, but by the comparison of their example” (Jerome). The aggravation, Barnes notes, is that “ignorant and wicked heathen repented when threatened… by a mere man — Jonah”; the Jews, threatened with eternal punishment “by the Son of God… repent not.” Henry numbers the ways Christ exceeds Jonah — the Son of God, not a man; come to his own; renewing his calls; preaching repentance with encouragement; confirming it with miracles — “yet the Ninevites repented… but the Jews were not wrought upon.”

The Latin fathers and Reformed draw out the doctrine. Remigius takes the verse to prove “one resurrection of the good and the bad,” against the heretics who taught two, and that judgment follows hard upon it. Jerome and Rabanus mark that “the faith of the Gentiles is significantly set above that of Israel.” Calvin states the contrast: “ungodly men… repented at the voice of an unknown… person… while this country, which is the sanctuary of heavenly doctrine, hears not the Son of God” — so “we are worse than all the unbelievers,” if Christ now speaking from heaven does not bring us to obey.

The witnesses sharpen the contrast between Nineveh and Jerusalem. The Ninevites, Henry notes, were “brought… to such a repentance as prevented their ruin” by “one short sermon… with no great solemnity” and “no miracle”; the Jews, hearing the Son of God who “wrought abundance of miracles… all miracles of mercy,” were “hardened in an unbelief that shall hasten their ruin.” Hence “the goodness of some, who have less helps… will aggravate the badness of those who have much greater”: those who “by the twilight discover the things… of their peace, will shame those who grope at noon-day.” Chrysostom presses that the Jews should not presume on a Nineveh-like deliverance: they “should have no fruit of the benefit of the passion, but should suffer… grievous things.” The greatness of Christ above Jonah — in person, errand, and grace — makes the rejection, the witnesses agree, the heavier in the day of judgment.
42:
On Matthew 12:42 — “the queen of the south shall rise up in the judgment… for she came from the uttermost parts of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon; and, behold, a greater than Solomon is here” — the witnesses read her zeal as a further reproach.

The grammatical-historical commentators identify her and her journey. She is the queen of Sheba (1 Kings 10:1), of Arabia or, with Josephus and Calvin, of Ethiopia; “the uttermost parts of the earth” is “a form of speech… signifying a great distance” (Clarke). Chrysostom marks that her example exceeds even Jonah’s Nineveh: “the queen of the south waited not for Solomon to come to her, but herself sought him… a woman, and a barbarian, and dwelling so far away.” Ellicott notes the chapter’s “marvellous assertions” of Christ’s majesty — “greater than the Temple… than Jonas… than Solomon: could this be rightly claimed by any man… who was not more than man?”

The Puritan Henry presses the comparison home in particulars: the queen had “no invitation,” came over “many difficulties,” sought but “philosophy and politics,” and could only “hear” Solomon’s wisdom; but we are “invited to Christ,” have him “among us,” may have “wisdom to salvation,” and he “will himself be made of God to us Wisdom.” So “if we do not hear the wisdom of Christ, the forwardness of the queen of Sheba… will rise up in judgment against us.” Calvin adds the contrast: “a woman who had not been at all educated in the school of God” came to an earthly king, “while the Jews… reject their… only teacher.”

The witnesses heap up the queen’s disadvantages to magnify the Jews’ guilt. She came, Henry notes, “from the uttermost parts of the earth,” a woman “unfit for travel,” a queen leaving her realm, over “a journey long and perilous,” on “uncertainties,” to hear only earthly “wisdom… philosophy and politics” — and could but “hear” Solomon, who “could not give her wisdom.” Against all this stands the privilege of those who reject Christ: “we are invited… to sit at his feet”; he is “wisdom itself, in whom are hid all the treasures of wisdom”; we “have Christ among us… he stands at the door, and knocks”; his is “wisdom to salvation,” and “he will himself be made of God to us Wisdom.” So the queen’s zeal “will rise up in judgment against us and condemn us”; for, as Chrysostom marks, “he spake of trees and wood, I of unspeakable mysteries.”
43:
On Matthew 12:43 — “when the unclean spirit is gone out of a man, he walketh through dry places, seeking rest, and findeth none” — the witnesses read the parable of the returning demon, applied to that generation.

The eastern and Latin fathers read it of the Jewish people. Chrysostom: as a freed demoniac, growing remiss, draws “more grievous illusions,” so the Jews — once delivered from the demon of idolatry by the prophets, then refusing Christ — “shall suffer more heavy calamities” under Vespasian and Titus. Jerome, against those who apply it to heretics, insists “the concluding words… compel us to refer it… to the Jewish people”: the spirit “went out from the Jews when they received the Law,” walked “through the wilderness of the Gentiles,” and finding no place when they believed, “returned to his house.” Remigius and Gregory read the “dry places” as “the hearts” — of the Gentiles lacking the moisture of Scripture, or of the righteous “dried from… carnal lust.”

The grammatical-historical and Reformed commentators draw the general truth. Barnes notes Christ “speaks according to the ancient opinions… that evil spirits had their abodes in… desolate… regions” (Revelation 18:2; Isaiah 13:21), the demon finding no “rest” out of the bosoms of men. Calvin makes the “going out” magnify grace — “the devil is cast out of us, whenever Christ shines upon us” — and warns that the enemy, “vanquished and put to flight by Christ,” only whets “his rage… to do us injury,” so that “as soon as Christ calls us, a sharper… contest is prepared” (1 Peter 5:8).

The witnesses weigh the parable as both a warning to the nation and a caution to every soul. The relapse described — the spirit returning to find his house “empty, swept, and garnished” — is, all agree, the picture of a reformation that stops short of Christ. Chrysostom applies it first to himself and us: “if after being enlightened and delivered… we are again possessed by the same wickedness, the punishment of these latter sins will be greater than of the first” (John 5:14). Calvin draws the doctrine of grace and of danger together: the casting out of the demon “magnifies the power… of the grace of God,” for “the devil is cast out of us, whenever Christ shines upon us”; yet, driven out, he “conceives resentment at having lost his prey” and “arouses all his senses to attack us anew” — so that “as soon as Christ calls us, a sharper… contest is prepared.”
44:
On Matthew 12:44 — “then he saith, I will return into my house from whence I came out; and… he findeth it empty, swept, and garnished” — the witnesses read the demon’s return to the unguarded soul.

The grammatical-historical commentators expound the figure. The man is called the demon’s “house”; “empty” of the former inhabitant and of good (Barnes, Clarke), “swept” of gross vices by an outward reformation, and “garnished” — but, as Clarke notes, garnished with “the vain showy trifles of folly,” the affections gadding idle. Ellicott reads the “two-fold symbolism”: the state of the man “left to… conventional morality, with no higher spiritual influence to protect” him, and of the nation he typifies. Robertson notes the demon’s irony, “as a man leaves his house to go for a walk.”

The Reformed Calvin makes the empty house the very thing Satan loves. Believers “in whom the Spirit… efficaciously dwells, are fortified on all sides,” but Satan “never finds a more appropriate habitation… than when, having parted with Christ, we receive Satan as a guest”; his “highest delight” is in “the emptiness which is produced, when man sets no value on the grace of God.” Wesley: the house is “empty of God… swept from… all the fruits of the Spirit, and garnished with levity and security,” so that “there is nothing to keep him out, and much to invite him in.” The misattached Cyril fragment, on the new birth of water and the Spirit, sits beside the verse.

The witnesses make the swept and garnished house the very emblem of an empty profession. Gill reads “my house” of the Pharisees and outward professors, who “notwithstanding their… pretensions to holiness, are Satan’s house still.” The peril, all agree, is reformation without indwelling: the soul cleansed of gross vice but “empty… of good works through slothfulness” (the Catena), “garnished… with feigned virtues through hypocrisy.” Calvin presses the warning home: Satan’s “highest delight” is “the emptiness which is produced, when man sets no value on the grace of God, and is indifferent about making a good use of it.” The remedy is that the cleansed abode be filled — that “the word of God in sound doctrine” be brought in as its dweller (Augustine) — for only the heart in which the Spirit “efficaciously dwells” is “fortified on all sides, so that no opening is left for Satan.”
45:
On Matthew 12:45 — “then goeth he, and taketh… seven other spirits more wicked than himself… and the last state of that man is worse than the first. Even so shall it be also unto this wicked generation” — the witnesses read the relapse and its application.

The Latin fathers draw out the “seven.” Jerome marks the Jews “now possessed by a larger number of daemons in blaspheming Jesus Christ… than… in Egypt before they had knowledge of the Law” — the sevenfold number set against the “seven spirits” of grace (Isaiah 11:2). Augustine reads the “seven other” as “sevenfold hypocrisy”: when the cleansed soul receives not “the word of God in sound doctrine,” the old lust returns and “makes the last state… worse.” The point, all the fathers agree, is the peril of an empty reformation.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan commentators fix the application on the nation. The closing words — “even so shall it be unto this wicked generation” — show, says Henry, that “sinners, when awakened, if they grieve away the Spirit of God, become worse than before”: their hearts hardened, “seven devils often dwell where one did.” Christ thus warns that the brief stir of conviction his preaching wrought, if it issued in no true cleansing by his indwelling, would leave the Jews more deeply possessed than at the first — the heavier judgment soon to fall in their ruin. The lesson, the witnesses agree, is universal: a soul reformed but not inhabited by Christ is an open door to a sevenfold return of evil.

The witnesses press the universal caution within the national warning. The seven returning spirits, “more wicked than himself,” figure how grievously a man or a people may sink who, once moved, refuse the indwelling Christ. Henry draws the lesson plainly: “sinners, when awakened, if they grieve away the Spirit of God, become worse than before” — “their hearts are hard, their consciences are seared… and they plunge deeper… into sin” — “seven devils often dwell where one did.” For the Jews, Christ’s preaching had been such a stirring, soon followed by harder unbelief, and so by a doom (in their ruin under Rome) worse than any former bondage. The closing word fastens it: “even so shall it be unto this wicked generation.” The witnesses leave the warning open to all: a house swept by conviction but left tenantless is more exposed than before, for “there is nothing to keep him out, and much to invite him in” (Wesley).
46:
On Matthew 12:46 — “while he yet talked… his mother and his brethren stood without, desiring to speak with him” — the witnesses note the interruption and its occasion.

The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene. The kinsmen came, Mark relates, thinking him “beside himself” (Mark 3:21); Clarke notes “they came to seize upon him, for they thought he was distracted.” The throng was so great, Robertson observes, that he was “not even stopping to eat” (Mark 3:20), and “it was hard for Mary… to leave Jesus with the excited throng.” The “brethren,” on the common view, were near kinsmen; they “stood without,” unable or unwilling to press through to him.

The witnesses read the moment as the setting for a great saying on spiritual kinship. Henry notes the verses present “Christ’s love for his followers”: that, “much as he loved his mother,… he loved his disciples more.” Calvin will judge that Mary “acted improperly in attempting to interrupt the progress of his discourse,” her eagerness reproved. The interruption, breaking in upon his teaching, gives occasion for Christ to disparage — in comparison — “the relationship of flesh and blood,” and to exalt the bond of faith. So a domestic incident becomes, in his hand, a revelation of who are nearest to him: not the kindred of nature merely, but the doers of the Father’s will.

The witnesses set the homely incident as the frame of a great truth. While Christ taught the throng, his kinsmen — come, Mark tells, supposing him “beside himself” — “stood without, desiring to speak with him.” The contrast Henry marks is striking: “in the midst of his greatest honours, he gave proofs of his humility,” and here proofs of his love, that “much as he loved his mother… he loved his disciples more.” Calvin will judge Mary to have “acted improperly in attempting to interrupt the progress of his discourse” — her motherly eagerness reproved. The verse is given, the witnesses agree, not to dishonour the natural ties — which Christ everywhere “observed and maintained in all sanctity” — but to make the occasion for declaring who are nearest to him. The interruption of flesh and blood becomes the doorway to the revelation of the family of faith.
47:
On Matthew 12:47 — “then one said unto him, Behold, thy mother and thy brethren stand without, desiring to speak with thee” — the witnesses note the message and a textual question.

The grammatical-historical commentators address the verse’s standing. Robertson reports that the leading manuscripts (Aleph, B, L, the Old Syriac) “omit this verse,” which “is genuine in Mark 3:32” and Luke 8:20, and “was probably copied into Matthew” from one of them. Gill harmonizes the reports — that “more than one” told him — by noting that, “upon his mother and brethren calling to him… first one and then another… might apprise him,” especially as “he did not immediately go out unto them.”

The message itself, the witnesses observe, is the occasion rather than the substance of the passage. The one who spoke meant, no doubt, a natural courtesy — that his nearest relations waited and desired him. But the delay in Christ’s going out, and the answer he gives, turn the moment to a higher purpose. The verse serves to set the stage: the claim of flesh and blood is announced, that Christ may, in the verses following, set above it the claim of those who do the will of God. Whether retained or, with the best texts, regarded as borrowed, the report carries the narrative to its point — the great question, “Who is my mother?”

The witnesses treat the message as the bridge to the saying. Whether the verse be retained or, with the best texts, regarded as borrowed from Mark and Luke (Robertson), its office is the same: to bring the natural claim before Christ that he may answer it. Gill’s harmonizing — that “first one and then another” told him, as “he did not immediately go out” — preserves the agreement of the Evangelists and explains the delay. That delay is itself significant: Christ does not hasten to the door at the call of kindred, but lets the moment ripen into teaching. The one who spoke meant a courtesy; Christ turns it to a revelation. So the report, slight in itself, carries the narrative to its height — the question “Who is my mother?” and the hand stretched toward the disciples — in which the bond of grace is set, in its own order, above the bond of blood.
48:
On Matthew 12:48 — “but he answered and said unto him that told him, Who is my mother? and who are my brethren?” — the witnesses guard the saying from misreading and draw out its doctrine.

The grammatical-historical commentators stress there was “no want of affection” in Christ. Barnes: his whole life proved his love for his mother (John 19:26–27); the question was asked “merely to fix the attention of the hearers… to show… who sustained… the nearest… relation” — the tie to his disciples being “more tender and sacred” than even that to his mother. Clarke recalls that they came supposing him “distracted” (Mark 3:21), which explains “this seeming disregard.”

The Reformed Calvin reads the words as reproving “Mary’s eagerness” — she “acted improperly in attempting to interrupt” his teaching — while teaching “a very useful doctrine.” By “disparaging the relationship of flesh and blood,” Christ “admits all his disciples… to the same honorable rank, as if they were his nearest relatives.” This is bound to his office: he is given “not to a small number… but to all the godly, who are united in one body with him by faith,” so that “there is no tie of relationship more sacred than spiritual relationship” (2 Corinthians 5:16). Wesley: “our Lord’s knowing why they came sufficiently justifies his seeming disregard of them.”

The witnesses guard the saying from every charge of coldness. There was “no want of affection or respect in Jesus towards his mother,” Barnes insists, “as is proved by his whole life” (Luke 2:51; John 19:26–27); the question was asked “merely to fix the attention of the hearers,” and to show “how great was his love for his disciples, when it was more than even that for his mother.” It is, he adds, “a bright illustration of his own doctrine” that we must prefer him to all earthly ties (Matthew 10:37). Calvin makes it “a very useful doctrine”: by “disparaging the relationship of flesh and blood,” Christ “admits all his disciples… to the same honorable rank” — teaching us “to behold Christ with the eyes of faith,” and that every one regenerated by the Spirit “is… admitted to the closest union with Christ.”
49:
On Matthew 12:49 — “and he stretched forth his hand toward his disciples, and said, Behold my mother and my brethren!” — the witnesses read the gesture and its bold exaltation of the spiritual family.

The grammatical-historical commentators mark both the act and the claim. Gill notes “by whom are meant, not only the twelve, but all others present, who truly believed… both men and women.” Robertson pictures “a dramatic wave of the hand”: Jesus “loved his mother and brothers, but they were not to interfere in his Messianic work,” his “real spiritual family” including “all who follow him.” Ellicott draws out that “spiritual relationships… are above” natural ones, and that when the two clash “the latter must of right prevail” (compare Matthew 10:37).

The witnesses note the verse’s controversial bearing. Ellicott observes the words “have naturally occupied a prominent position” in the Protestant protest against “the idolatrous worship of the Virgin Mother”: they “exclude the thought that her intercession is mightier… than that of any other… saintly soul,” a protest “all the stronger because of the absence of” any polemical aim. Wesley sees in it “the highest severity, and the highest goodness — severity to his natural, goodness to his spiritual relations.” The lesson all draw is that nearness to Christ is reckoned not by blood but by faith and obedience — the disciples, by his own word, made his mother and his brethren.

The witnesses dwell on the bold tenderness of the gesture. The wave of the hand toward the disciples (Robertson) names as Christ’s “mother and brethren” not the twelve only but “all others present, who truly believed… both men and women” (Gill). Wesley reads in it “the highest severity, and the highest goodness” — “severity to his natural, goodness to his spiritual relations.” Ellicott draws out the controversial weight: the words “exclude the thought that” the Virgin’s “intercession is mightier… than that of any other… saintly soul,” standing as a protest “all the stronger because of the absence of” any polemical aim against “the excess of reverence which has passed into a cultus.” The lesson all draw is the same: nearness to Christ is reckoned by faith, not blood — the lowliest believer, by his own word, made “dearer than mother, and sisters, and brothers” (Henry).
50:
On Matthew 12:50 — “for whosoever shall do the will of my Father which is in heaven, the same is my brother, and sister, and mother” — the witnesses read the ground of spiritual kinship with Christ.

The grammatical-historical commentators define the “doing.” Gill is careful that it is not “perfect obedience… to the righteous law” — else “no one could be in such a… relation” — but “the obedience of faith… to believe in Christ” (John 6:40). Barnes: this is “what Christ recognises as the ground of a spiritual relationship… not the mere fact of baptism, but that which baptism signifies… the doing the will of God.” Ellicott notes the special mention of “sister” suggests that those bearing that name “had joined the mother and the brethren” in the attempt to interrupt him — yet the doer of God’s will is made “as dear to the heart of Christ as was the mother whom He loved.”

The Reformed Calvin guards the title from misuse: it does not mean men “fulfill, in a perfect manner, the whole righteousness of the law,” but commends “faith, which is the source… of holy obedience, and… covers the defects… of the flesh.” Christ, though he “paid the strictest attention to” his lawful duties to relatives, teaches that “in comparison of spiritual relationship… little” is due to the flesh; and since he bestows “the inestimable honor of being reckoned as his brethren,” we are “guilty of the basest ingratitude” if we do not “direct every effort towards this object.” Clarke: “what an encouragement for fervent attachment to God!”

The witnesses close the chapter on the ground of all spiritual kinship. To “do the will of my Father,” Gill insists, is “not… perfect obedience… to the law” — else “no one could be in such a… relation” — but “the obedience of faith… to believe in Christ” (John 6:40). Calvin guards the title likewise: Christ commends “faith, which is the source… of holy obedience, and… covers the defects… of the flesh.” Barnes makes it the test of true discipleship — “not the mere fact of baptism, but that which baptism signifies” — and Henry finds in the whole passage the abiding comfort of Christ’s love: though his people be “poor, and despised, and unknown to the rich and mighty, yet to Jesus they are still dearer than mother, and sisters, and brothers.” And since he bestows so “inestimable” an honour, Calvin warns, “we must be held guilty of the basest ingratitude, if we do not disregard all the desires of the flesh, and direct every effort towards this object.”
